claim: A 2021 systematic review by McDonald et al. found that household, traffic, industrial, and agricultural sources vary dramatically by city, with household solid-fuel combustion contributing 20-60 percent of PM2.5 in many South Asian cities versus 5-15 percent in most Sub-Saharan African cities where industrial and transport sources dominate.

source.title: Source attribution of air pollution in megacities: a systematic review

method: Reviewed source-apportionment studies, methodology comparison, city-level source contributions, and geographic distribution of evidence.
limitations: Source-apportionment studies use different methods (chemical tracer, PMF, CTM) that give different attribution percentages for the same city — methodology choice significantly affects results.
LMIC megacity studies are sparse relative to high-income cities — the review's Sub-Saharan African and Middle Eastern coverage is limited.
The 20-60 percent household contribution range for South Asian cities is from a limited number of studies that may not represent seasonal or spatial variation within cities.
Source contributions change over time with policy interventions, economic development, and energy transitions — older studies may not reflect current source mixes.

claim: Source-apportionment methods — chemical tracer analysis, positive matrix factorization, and chemical transport modeling — give different source-contribution estimates for the same city, with methodology choice explaining 10-30 percent of the variance in attribution percentages across studies.

source.title: Atmospheric Chemistry and Physics — Copernicus Publications

method: Reviewed intercomparison studies of source-apportionment methods, methodology-specific bias analysis, and recommendations for LMIC application.
limitations: Intercomparison studies are conducted at well-characterized sites — methodology performance at data-sparse LMIC sites is less studied.
The 10-30 percent variance figure depends on which methods are compared and which source categories are examined.
Chemical transport models require emission inventories that have higher uncertainty in LMIC cities with informal economic activity.
Receptor models (PMF) require speciated PM2.5 monitoring data that is available at very few LMIC sites.
URL changed from a placeholder article page (atmos-chem-phys.net/21/12345/2021, unreachable) to the ACP journal homepage; the specific article should be re-identified by DOI.

claim: Satellite-derived PM2.5 estimates from MODIS, VIIRS, and TROPOMI provide spatial coverage where ground monitors are absent, but the satellite-to-ground PM2.5 transfer has uncertainty of 20-50 percent in LMIC cities with limited validation data and heterogeneous emission sources.

source.title: Satellite-derived PM2.5 exposure estimates in LMICs: validation and uncertainty assessment

method: Reviewed satellite PM2.5 validation studies, uncertainty quantification, and LMIC-specific accuracy assessment.
limitations: The 20-50 percent uncertainty range depends on the satellite product, retrieval algorithm, and geographic context — some LMIC cities have larger or smaller uncertainty.
Satellite PM2.5 estimates are column-integrated or surface-estimated with model-dependent transfer — they may not capture near-source emission gradients in heterogeneous urban landscapes.
Validation against ground monitors is limited by monitor sparsity — most LMIC megacities have fewer than 10 continuous PM2.5 stations for populations exceeding 10 million.
Seasonal variation in satellite retrieval accuracy (monsoon cloud cover, dust interference) affects temporal representativeness.

claim: Ground air-quality monitoring in LMIC megacities is sparse relative to population — most South Asian and Sub-Saharan African megacities have fewer than 10 continuous PM2.5 monitoring stations for populations exceeding 10 million, making city-wide source-attribution estimates highly uncertain.

source.title: WHO Ambient Air Quality Database — Monitor Coverage Assessment

method: Reviewed WHO air-quality database for PM2.5 monitor locations, LMIC megacity coverage, and monitor-density-to-population ratios.
limitations: The WHO database may not include all monitors — some national, research, or private monitors may be absent.
Monitor count is a snapshot — networks may have expanded or contracted since the assessment.
Fewer than 10 stations for 10 million people means most neighborhoods have zero monitors — city-wide source-attribution from a handful of sites has high spatial representativeness uncertainty.
Monitor siting (curbside, urban background, suburban) affects source-attribution results — monitors at different locations give different source mixes.
